extraction_result.cfp_subject: Funding opportunity RFA-AI-25-006, 'Molecular Mechanisms of Combination Adjuvants (MMCA) (R01 Clinical Trial Not Allowed)', supports research to define the molecular and immunological mechanisms of two or more vaccine adjuvants working in concert. The program aims to improve rational vaccine design by predicting immune profiles elicited by combination adjuvants, focusing on MOA studies leading to long-term protective immunity against infectious diseases. Excludes clinical trials, single adjuvant studies, and research on AIDS/HIV/SIV.

content.title: Formatting requirement
subtitles: Applicants must follow instructions in the Research (R) Instructions in the How to Apply - Application Guide, unless otherwise specified in this NOFO or NIH Guide for Grants and Contracts.
Conformance to all requirements in both the Application Guide and the NOFO is required and strictly enforced.
Applicants must read and follow all application instructions in the Application Guide and any program-specific instructions in Section IV.
When program-specific instructions deviate from the Application Guide, program-specific instructions must be followed.
Applications that do not comply with instructions may be delayed or not accepted for review.
All applications are due by 5:00 PM local time of applicant organization.
Applicants are encouraged to apply early to allow adequate time for corrections to errors found during submission process by the due date.

content.title: Explicit requirement
subtitles: Research studies must focus on two or more vaccine adjuvants (combination adjuvants) to understand their mechanisms of action in concert.
All adjuvants considered must have previously demonstrated immune modulating activity.

Studies must explore the mechanism(s) of action (MOA) of combination adjuvants that have already been shown individually to be effective in enhancing or modulating immune responses when compared with antigen alone.
At least one adjuvant combination must be proposed for examination.
Applicants should include a strong rationale for combining adjuvants based on their individual immunostimulatory properties and/or effects in previously studied systems.
Applications including studies on AIDS, HIV, or SIV will be considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed.
Applications including projects that do not examine immune mechanisms in animal models will be considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed.

Applications including projects that only examine immune mechanisms in standard inbred and germ-free mouse lines without validation and/or further mechanistic examination using more complex model systems (e.g., Collaborative Cross mice, microbial experienced 'dirty' mice, humanized mice, other relevant animal models, in vitro human models) will be considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed.
Applications including projects that use only one adjuvant without combining it with at least one additional adjuvant will be considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed.
Applications including clinical trials will be considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed.
A minimum of $50,000 in direct costs must be set aside during each year of research, beginning in Year 2, for collaborative projects amongst the MMCA investigators.
Investigators should not include or propose specific collaborative projects within their application.
Grant recipients are required to attend a kickoff meeting and subsequent annual meetings organized by NIAID to present results, identify new collaborations, and share materials.

content.title: Part 1. Overview Information - Title only
subtitles: Participating Organization(s): National Institutes of Health (NIH)
Components of Participating Organizations: National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID)
Funding Opportunity Title: Molecular Mechanisms of Combination Adjuvants (MMCA) (R01 Clinical Trial Not Allowed)
Activity Code: R01 Research Project Grant
Announcement Type: Reissue of RFA-AI-20-004

Related Notices: Updates on agency priorities, grant application and review changes for due dates on or after January 25, 2025, implementation changes for Genomic Data Sharing Plans, and implementation details for the NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy.
Funding Opportunity Number (FON): RFA-AI-25-006
Companion Funding Opportunity: None
Number of Applications: See Part 2, Section III. 3. Additional Information on Eligibility.
Assistance Listing Number(s): 93.855

Funding Opportunity Purpose: To support research studies of two or more vaccine adjuvants to understand their mechanisms of action in concert, with the long-term goal of improving rational vaccine design.

Funding Opportunity Goal(s): To assist public and private nonprofit institutions and individuals in establishing, expanding, and improving biomedical research and training in infectious diseases and related areas, encompassing both basic and clinical research.

content.title: Key Dates - Title only
subtitles: Posted Date: January 13, 2025
Open Date (Earliest Submission Date): May 10, 2025
Letter of Intent Due Date(s): 30 days prior to the application due date
Application Due Dates: June 10, 2025 for New, Renewal, Resubmission, and Revision applications.
Review and Award Cycles: Scientific Merit Review in November 2025, Advisory Council Review in January 2026, Earliest Start Date in April 2026.
Expiration Date: June 11, 2025
Due Dates for E.O. 12372: Not Applicable
Required Application Instructions: Applicants must follow instructions in the Research (R) Instructions in the How to Apply - Application Guide, with program-specific instructions taking precedence.

content.title: Section I. Notice of Funding Opportunity Description - Title only
subtitles: Background: Discusses the benefits of subunit vaccines, the need for adjuvants, and the challenges and potential of combination adjuvants.

Research Objectives: Supports research to define molecular and immunological mechanisms triggered by adjuvant combinations leading to long-term protective immunity against infectious diseases, focusing on MOA studies of effective combination adjuvants. MOA studies may include, but are not limited to: quantitative and qualitative assessments of antibody production, CD4 and CD8 T cell activation and function, induction and maintenance of innate and adaptive immune memory, tissue-specific immunity, studies of signaling pathways, characterization of innate immunity, pathogen challenge studies, systems immunology analyses, and safety studies.
